OM-CDR-BRIEF · v1.2
MODELLED POSITIONS OMM → SGP4/SDP4 LOCAL-FIRST LOCATION NO CAMERA-DETECTION CLAIM
Critical Design Review · Executive & Development Brief v1.2

OrbitMark

A controlled development baseline for an honest, model-based AR sky companion that lets people identify, save, annotate and follow catalogued satellites, rocket bodies and trackable debris.

APPROVE WITH CONDITIONS
Product wedge: personal orbital catalog
Release authority: CDR v1.1 + Appendix G
AuthorityFinal product-development baseline
Primary platformsiOS + Android via Capacitor
Core computationOn-device orbital worker
Launch postureBootstrap-first · raise-ready
Brief map
00Basis & control 01CDR decision 02Product definition 03Market & competitors 04UX/UI authority 05Architecture 06Orbital data engine 07Passes & trust 08Privacy, security, a11y 09Verification & gates 10Team execution 11Business model 12Risks & open items 13Source basis
Truth rule The camera does not detect debris. OrbitMark renders a computed direction from orbital elements, time and observer location.
§00

Basis, authority and evidence control

This page condenses the 66-page CDR v1.1 and selected commercial context from the business plan. It is an orientation and handoff artifact; the controlled CDR remains authoritative.

FVerified fact DApproved design decision RTestable requirement TProject threshold OOpen item
Authority order

CDR first

CDR → approved contracts → tokens and screen specifications → diagrams → PDR → business plan.

Change control

No silent drift

Changes to the wedge, canonical orbital model, providers, location handling, claims or child-directed use require an impact note and re-review.

External sign-off

Still required

Provider terms, legal/privacy, accessibility, security and numerical validation are gates—not implied approvals.

Non-negotiable product claim

OrbitMark shows modelled positions with explicit uncertainty. It must never imply that a phone camera has detected, photographed or uniquely identified a piece of orbital debris.

§01

CDR decision

Proceed with development, but only through evidence-based gates. Camera Sky, pass alerts and public orbital-data refresh are blocked until the dedicated orbital-data authorization package is accepted.

GO
M0/M1 technical and product work may start.
G0.5
Mandatory orbital ingestion, identity, propagation and rollback evidence gate.
NO
No production release based on visual plausibility or “it looks right.”
18
Controlled screen contracts, including complete non-camera fallback paths.

Conditions that control development

  • OMM-compatible JSON is canonical; TLE is compatibility input only.
  • Object history is immutable; packages publish atomically with rollback.
  • SGP4/SDP4 calculations run in a worker with explicit frames and units.
  • Precise observer location stays on-device by default.
  • Manual Sky provides a full useful experience without camera or motion sensors.
  • Candidate lists replace false single-object certainty.
  • Accessibility, failure states and truthful copy are release requirements.
  • Golden vectors, failure injection and supported-device profiling are required evidence.
§02

Approved product definition

OrbitMark is not another “largest satellite catalog” viewer. The AR/manual sky is the discovery surface; the personal catalog is the product.

TonightImmediate value, next pass, overhead objects and freshness.
→
Choose SkyCamera path or complete Manual Sky path.
→
Tap a regionFind bounded candidates—not a fake pinpoint.
→
UnderstandIdentity, source, epoch, geometry and visibility state.
→
Save & annotateTag, note, watch and build a personal orbital catalog.
→
ReturnPass alerts, catalog updates, learning and sharing.
MVP includes

Useful, honest and local-first

  • Tonight, Camera Sky and Manual Sky.
  • Candidate list and object detail.
  • Personal catalog with tags, notes and watch state.
  • Local pass calculations and local notifications.
  • Search, filters, education and data-status screens.
Explicitly deferred

Not in the launch thesis

  • Collision/conjunction assessment or operator workflows.
  • Claims about detecting sub-centimetre or untracked debris.
  • Mandatory accounts, public social feeds or precise-location storage.
  • 3D globe as a substitute for proving the curation loop.
  • Analyst-object persistence without durable identity evidence.
§03

Market reality and competitive position

The space-debris problem supplies relevance and education value, but the monetizable category is consumer astronomy and informal STEM—not government space situational awareness.

~40,000Tracked/catalogued objects in the authoritative public context; only catalogued objects can be drawn individually.
~1.2MEstimated debris larger than 1 cm; most cannot be individually tracked or pointed to.
~2%Business-plan base conversion assumption, close to cited freemium medians—not a guarantee.
$29.99Pro annual price anchor in the business-plan scenario model.

The occupied category

Mature apps already provide AR sky views, satellite tracking, alerts and large catalogs. Competing on “more objects” or generic AR is structurally weak.

Personal curation depth ↑ Satellite / debris specialization → SkyView Stellarium SkySafari Orbitrack Satellite Tracker Heavens-Above OrbitMark

The wedge

Viewer → curation tool

Personal orbital catalog

Save an object, add tags and notes, choose watch state, receive passes, revisit the object and later share a curated collection.

Defensible position

Pair the personal-catalog loop with transparent source age, uncertainty and an orbital-sustainability education frame.

Do not position on

Catalog size, pinpoint phone accuracy, operator-grade SSA or the ability to “see debris through the camera.”

Competitor reference matrix

ProductAR / trackingSatellite & debris scopePersonal annotationPlan pricing reference*
SkyViewARStars, ISS, “space junk”NoFree / ~US$2
Stellarium MobileARFull sky + satellitesNoFree / US$13.99 Plus
SkySafariARSatellites + telescope controlNoUS$4.99–17.99 + annual premium
Star Walk / Satellite TrackerARLarge satellite catalogNoFree + IAP / subscription
OrbitrackAR + VRVisible man-made objectsNo~US$4.99 one-time
Heavens-AboveTrackerISS and large catalogNoFree + Pro
ISS DetectorAlertsISS, Starlink and radio objectsNoFree + subscription
OrbitMarkAR + ManualCatalogued satellites, rocket bodies and trackable debrisCore wedgeFree / Pro US$29.99 / education

*Commercial figures are June 2026 business-plan references and must be rechecked before external publication or pricing decisions.

§04

UX/UI design authority

The interface must make uncertainty understandable without making the product feel broken. It earns trust by showing what is known, what is calculated and what remains unknown.

Principle 01

Value before permission

Onboarding demonstrates “what is overhead” and the personal-catalog loop before requesting camera, motion, location or notifications.

Principle 02

Uncertainty is visible

Use rings, candidate lists, data age and plain-language confidence states instead of false coordinate precision.

Principle 03

Manual parity

Every key task—browse, identify candidates, save, review and schedule passes—must remain available without camera access.

Representative screen direction

01Tonight

Value-first home with data freshness, the next watched pass and a direct route into Sky.

02Camera Sky

Modelled markers, uncertainty rings, confidence state and a manual calibration escape hatch.

03Candidate List

A region tap returns ranked candidates; the UI does not pretend a single pinpoint identification.

04Object Detail

Identity, source, element age, next pass, visibility wording and the save-to-catalog action.

05My Catalog

The wedge: a personal, tagged, watched and later shareable collection of orbital objects.

06Passes

Local pass predictions and alert state for watched objects, with explicit visibility caveats.

Critical design contracts

SurfaceMust communicateMust not do
TonightNext watched pass, data age, observer/time state, honest empty state.Use “live” for computed orbital data.
Camera / Manual SkyModelled time, confidence, clock warning, candidate region and unavailable state.Imply camera detection or a single certain match.
Candidate ListAngular ordering, object type and identity limitations.Silently select the nearest marker.
Object DetailCatalog/source ID, element epoch, package version, calculation time and visibility state.Turn “sunlit” into an unsupported “visible” claim.
PassesSource age, local-time conversion, alert caveat, stale/skew suppression.Schedule alerts from stale or invalid state.
Data StatusLast success, active package, fallback, provider delay, quarantine and support export.Collapse every failure into “something went wrong.”
§05

Approved system architecture

A local-first mobile architecture separates persistence, orbital computation, orientation, presentation and backend operations. IndexedDB is persistence—not a frame-loop data source.

Mobile shell

React/Vite + Capacitor; Tonight, Sky, Catalog, Passes, Learn and Settings.

Orbital worker

OMM parsing, SatRec creation, SGP4/SDP4, coordinate transforms, working-set scheduling and diagnostics.

Backend

Versioned APIs, immutable orbital history, package publishing, optional catalog sync and operational status.

Architecture correction from the preliminary design

satellite.js is used in a TypeScript Web Worker. The approved baseline does not call it a WASM propagator, and the animation frame never performs full-catalog propagation or IndexedDB reads.

§06

Orbital object ingestion, identity and position engine

The system does not ingest “live positions.” It ingests orbital element sets and deterministically calculates a modelled state for a requested UTC time and observer location, then combines that result with an independently measured device pose.

1 — Ingestion and publication

Conditional fetchProvider-friendly ETag / Last-Modified requests.
→
ParseOMM-compatible JSON with raw payload retention.
→
ValidateSchema, ranges, finite values and propagation smoke tests.
→
Resolve identityStable object key, source IDs and analyst-object policy.
→
Persist immutablyRaw record, normalized set, lineage and ingest run.
→
Publish atomicallyVersioned package, manifest, checksum and last-known-good pointer.

Canonical object record

object_key · catalog_id · source · object_type · epoch · mean_motion · eccentricity · inclination · RAAN · argument_of_pericenter · mean_anomaly · BSTAR · provenance

Quarantine triggers

Parse failure, invalid/non-finite fields, identity instability, failed propagation, manifest/checksum failure or provider-policy breach.

2 — Position at any requested time

State(t) = SGP4 / SDP4(element_set, t − epoch)
position_teme_km + velocity_teme_km_s
→ Earth-fixed coordinates using GMST at UTC t
→ observer topocentric azimuth / elevation / range
→ angular separation from device orientation
→ bounded marker + uncertainty region + candidate list
Select the valid element set

Choose the latest eligible OMM set at or before the calculation time, preserving source, epoch, lineage and age.

Initialize SGP4/SDP4

Convert normalized mean elements to a deterministic SatRec; the implementation selects the near-Earth or deep-space branch.

Propagate to UTC t

Compute TEME position and velocity from elapsed minutes since the element epoch; reject library errors and non-finite output.

Rotate to Earth-fixed

Apply the approved GMST-based TEME→ECF contract for the same UTC instant and retain explicit frame and unit names.

Build the observer vector

Convert WGS-84 latitude, longitude and ellipsoidal height to ECEF, then subtract it from the object vector.

Solve local look angles

Transform the relative vector into East–North–Up and derive true azimuth, elevation and slant range.

Align device and camera

Fuse device attitude, screen rotation, true-north correction and camera boresight calibration into one orientation transform.

Project, rank and bound

Project only in-front objects, calculate angular separation, apply an uncertainty envelope and return a stable candidate set.

Deterministic calculation contract

ALG-01
Element-set selection
For each stable object identity, select the newest validated element set whose epoch is not in the future beyond the allowed clock tolerance. If the active package is invalid, incompatible or outside the object-class freshness policy, do not silently extrapolate: use the last-known-good package, show a degraded state or suppress the marker/alert according to policy.
ALG-02
OMM normalization and SatRec initialization
Normalize angles, units, epoch and identifiers; preserve the raw provider record; validate 0 ≤ eccentricity < 1, finite mean motion and legal angular fields; initialize the SGP4 record once per active element set. SatRec objects are cached inside the worker and are never rebuilt inside the animation frame.
ALG-03
SGP4 / SDP4 propagation
Use elapsed UTC minutes from the element epoch. The output contract is position_teme_km and velocity_teme_km_s. The application does not substitute a two-body Kepler model, numerical orbit integration or machine-learning prediction. Propagation errors, decay conditions and non-finite vectors become typed failure states.
ALG-04
TEME to Earth-fixed rotation
Calculate Greenwich mean sidereal time for the same UTC instant and rotate the TEME position into the MVP Earth-fixed frame. This is a declared consumer-grade transform, not an operator-grade ITRF/EOP solution. Cross-engine golden vectors quantify its contribution to the total error budget.
ALG-05
WGS-84 observer conversion
Convert the observer’s geodetic latitude φ, longitude λ and ellipsoidal height h to ECEF using WGS-84. Keep precise coordinates in the client process; cache only the minimum state needed for local use and obey location-accuracy and permission states.
ALG-06
Topocentric look-angle solution
Subtract observer ECEF from object ECF, rotate the relative vector into the local East–North–Up basis, then derive true azimuth, elevation and slant range. Objects below the configured horizon mask remain outside the normal visible working set unless needed for an imminent rise calculation.
ALG-07
Device-pose and north alignment
The orientation service outputs one normalized quaternion plus accuracy metadata. Use platform true heading where reliable; otherwise correct magnetic heading with a local geomagnetic-declination model. Apply display rotation and a calibrated camera-boresight offset. Poor heading accuracy widens the uncertainty region or forces Manual Sky.
ALG-08
Camera projection and culling
Convert the object’s ENU unit vector into camera coordinates using the inverse device/camera rotation. Reject vectors behind the camera and outside the field-of-view margin. Project accepted vectors using measured or calibrated camera intrinsics; a generic FOV approximation is allowed only in a labelled degraded mode.
ALG-09
Candidate matching
A tap defines an angular cone. Return every eligible object inside the cone plus its uncertainty envelope. Ordering is deterministic: angular separation first, then persistent identity eligibility, watched/saved priority, element freshness and stable catalog ID. There is no hidden visual-recognition model.
ALG-10
Multi-rate working-set scheduler
Run a low-frequency coarse catalog scan to select objects that are watched, near/above the horizon, near the camera field or approaching a pass. Propagate only this bounded active set at the benchmark-approved worker cadence. The UI consumes immutable snapshots and interpolates direction vectors between them.

Reference equations and frame conventions

Elapsed time: Δt_min = (t_UTC − epoch_UTC) / 60 s
Propagation: (r_TEME, v_TEME) = SGP4(SatRec, Δt_min)
MVP Earth rotation: r_ECF = R₃(θ_GMST(t_UTC)) · r_TEME

WGS-84 observer:
N(φ) = a / √(1 − e² sin²φ)
x_o = (N+h) cosφ cosλ
y_o = (N+h) cosφ sinλ
z_o = (N(1−e²)+h) sinφ

Relative vector: ρ_ECF = r_object_ECF − r_observer_ECF
east = −sinλ·ρx + cosλ·ρy
north = −sinφ cosλ·ρx − sinφ sinλ·ρy + cosφ·ρz
up = cosφ cosλ·ρx + cosφ sinλ·ρy + sinφ·ρz
azimuth = normalize₀₋₂π(atan2(east, north))
elevation = atan2(up, √(east²+north²))
range = √(east²+north²+up²)

Angular match: α = acos(clamp(d_camera · d_object, −1, 1))
Display envelope: U = clamp(U_orbit + U_orientation + U_clock + U_location + U_projection, U_min, U_max)
U is a conservative product envelope calibrated by validation; it is not presented as a statistical 95% confidence interval because public GP element sets do not provide a usable covariance for this purpose.

Worker scheduling and interpolation

function solveSkySnapshot(tUtc, observer, devicePose, activePackage): assert packageCompatible(activePackage) assert clockStateWithinPolicy(tUtc) if coarseScanDue(tUtc): coarseStates = propagateEligibleCatalog(tUtc, observer) activeSet = rankAndBound( watchedObjects, objectsNearHorizon, objectsInsideExpandedFov, objectsWithImminentPass, coarseStates ) for object in activeSet: satrec = satrecCache.getOrCreate(object.activeElementSet) teme = propagateSgp4(satrec, tUtc) ecf = rotateTemeToEcf(teme.position, gmst(tUtc)) look = solveEnuLookAngles(ecf, observer) uncertainty = buildConservativeEnvelope(object, look, devicePose) snapshot.add(object, look, uncertainty) publishImmutableSnapshot(snapshot) # Render thread: latest = workerSnapshot() direction = interpolateUnitVectors(latest.previous, latest.current, frameTime) screenPoint = projectWithCameraIntrinsics(direction, devicePose) drawOnlyWhen(screenPoint.inFront && screenPoint.insideFovMargin)

Interpolation rule

Interpolate normalized direction vectors—or unwrap angles before interpolation. Never linearly interpolate raw azimuth across the 359°→0° boundary. Recompute rather than interpolate when the snapshot gap exceeds the approved limit.

Sensor / orbit separation

Orbital look angles and phone pose are independent state streams. A fast pose update can move a marker without rerunning SGP4; a new orbital snapshot can update object direction without changing the sensor model.

Failure behaviour

Invalid package, propagation error, excessive element age, clock skew, unavailable true heading or low pose accuracy produces an explicit state: suppress, widen, recalibrate or switch to Manual Sky. The system does not freeze the last marker as if it were current.

Performance tuning

Coarse-scan cadence, active-set cap, propagation frequency and interpolation interval are configuration values fixed by supported-device profiling. They are not hard-coded assumptions in the product brief.

Algorithms deliberately not used for the position claim: camera computer vision, image recognition, GPS reception from the orbital object, Kalman filtering of an observed satellite track, a pure Kepler two-body orbit, or full-catalog propagation inside the animation frame.

Explicit frames

Internal names must preserve TEME/ECF/topocentric semantics and units. Generic “ECI” naming is not sufficient for cross-engine verification.

Time authority

Element epoch, package fetch time, requested UTC time and device clock skew are distinct and visible states.

Bounded workload

A coarse scan selects an active set. The UI interpolates results; it never propagates the full catalog on every animation frame.

3 — Identity rules

MVP exclusion

Temporary or analyst objects whose public identifiers may be reused are not eligible for persistent user annotations unless a durable lineage model is proven and migration-tested.

§07

Pass prediction, visibility and trust states

A pass is solved locally from elevation over time. “Above the horizon,” “sunlit” and “visible to the eye” are different states and must not be collapsed.

SampleAdaptive coarse samples of elevation(t).
→
BracketDetect rise/set sign changes against the horizon mask.
→
RefineBisection to the project tolerance.
→
MaximizeBounded search for maximum elevation.
→
QualifySunlit state, source age, clock and visibility status.
→
ScheduleRolling local notifications and rescheduling.
Geometry

Above horizon

A mathematical direction relative to the observer.

Illumination

Sunlit / shadow

Whether the object is illuminated by the Sun.

Brightness

Known / unknown

Brightness cannot be invented from geometry alone.

User wording

Likely visible

Only when the evidence and approved rule support it.

Preferred user copy

“Modelled above the horizon. Visual visibility is unknown.” This is more accurate than “You can see this debris now.”

§08

Privacy, security, accessibility and policy

Trust is implemented through data minimization, accessible fallback paths, controlled claims and verifiable operational behaviour.

Privacy

Location stays local

Precise coordinates are used for on-device look angles and pass calculations; they are not sent to the server by default.

Accessibility

No sensor dependency

Manual Sky, text lists, large targets, semantic labels, non-colour status and reduced-motion support are critical-path requirements.

Security

Provider & package integrity

Credentials remain server-side; packages use checksums, compatibility rules, atomic activation and rollback.

Control areaRelease obligation
AccessibilityWCAG 2.2 AA / platform accessibility testing, screen-reader flow, dynamic text, contrast, focus and no motion-only interaction.
AnalyticsNo precise location, free-form notes or raw sensor streams in product analytics.
Children / educationEducation licensing must not silently become a child-data product; a directed-minor use case requires separate governance.
SecurityOWASP MASVS-oriented review, secret scanning, dependency controls, penetration testing and a threat model before public scale.
Provider termsRate limits, credentials, redistribution and derived-data rights are verified before production.
ClaimsEvery accuracy, visibility, freshness and “real-time” statement is controlled by the claims registry.
§09

Verification, validation and release gates

The product is approved by numerical, operational, usability and accessibility evidence—not by visual inspection.

Gate G0.5 evidence
  • Provider integration, backoff and no credential leakage.
  • Identity reuse and analyst-object exclusion fixtures.
  • Immutable history, idempotency and no overwrite.
  • Manifest/checksum, interrupted-write and rollback tests.
  • Cross-engine numerical reference vectors.
Pre-beta evidence
  • Supported-device performance and battery profiles.
  • Pass rise/max/set parity and clock-skew simulation.
  • Failure-state UX and recovery testing.
  • Accessibility audit and critical-task usability study.
  • Security, privacy, legal/provider and claims reviews.

Evidence layers

Numerical
Stop-ship
Data lifecycle
Stop-ship
Accessibility
Stop-ship
Security/privacy
Stop-ship
Usability
Gate target
Commercial KPIs
Pivot test

Bar lengths communicate control priority, not completion status.

§10

Team execution sequence

Development teams are activated in dependency order so screen implementation cannot outrun the data and trust contracts it must display.

OrderWorkstreamRequired deliverableUnlocks
01Orbit / backendCanonical schemas, source adapter, identity rules, fixtures and ingest state machine.Publisher and QA
02Platform / backendImmutable package/manifest, API/status surfaces and operations runbook.Client cache and worker
03Orbital clientSatRec adapter, explicit frames/units, working-set worker and diagnostics.Sky and object UI
04Pass / mobilePass solver, freshness/clock rules and local notification lifecycle.Passes screens
05UX / contentAll state, copy, permission, a11y and fallback contracts.Frontend implementation
06QA / security / opsIndependent vectors, failure injection, profiles, reviews and evidence pack.G0.5 and beta approval
Claude Design / Claude Code handoff

Design and implementation must consume the CDR by authority order, preserve screen IDs and states, generate tokens/contracts first and log every deviation. Novel visual ideas are allowed; weakened claims, hidden failure states and invented scope are not.

§11

Business model and economic constraints

The product can be inexpensive to operate, but the acquisition economics reject a paid-growth-led plan. Organic, viral, education and PR channels are not optional—they are the model.

$42
Estimated net lifetime value per paying user in the base model.
$0.85
Estimated blended value per install at ~2% conversion.
$1.20–4.70
Cited CPI benchmark range; above blended install value.
~97%
Scenario education gross margin before sales effort.

Revenue architecture

Pro subscription
Core
Education
High value
Ads / affiliate
Light
Acquisition rule

Paid user acquisition is rejected until measured cohort economics prove positive payback. The base plan assumes organic, PR, sharing and education distribution.

Five-year scenario outputs

Bear revenue
$0.36M
Base revenue
$1.21M
Bull revenue
$3.22M
Base Y5 paid
34.1k
Base institutions
~280

Scenario estimates from the business plan; they are not forecasts or guarantees. Base cumulative five-year EBITDA is ~US$0.90M on a founder-sweat-equity basis.

§12

Principal risks, controls and open items

The CDR turns the most dangerous uncertainties into explicit controls and review items rather than burying them in assumptions.

RiskWhy it mattersControl / decision
Weak differentiationAR satellite viewers already exist.Measure the catalog/annotation loop; do not compete on generic sky rendering.
Sensor inaccuracyPhone orientation can be several degrees wrong.Uncertainty rings, candidate lists, calibration, manual mode and device evidence.
Stale element setsPrediction quality decays and manoeuvres break assumptions.Visible epoch/source age, class-specific guardrails, suppression and last-known-good packages.
Unstable identityReused analyst IDs could attach notes to the wrong object.Exclude from persistence until durable lineage is demonstrated.
Provider / terms failureData refresh may be blocked or redistribution restricted.Conditional fetch, backoff, source lanes, legal review and fallback.
Negative paid acquisitionBlended install value is below cited CPI.Organic-first launch; cohort-based growth gates and kill/pivot criteria.
Education governanceInstitutional use may introduce child-data obligations.No child-directed product without separate governance, data flow and policy review.
Open G-O-01

Element-age guardrails

Set by orbit/object class from validation evidence—not one invented universal limit.

Open G-O-02

Working-set cadence

Counts, update rates and interpolation limits come from supported-device profiling.

Open G-O-05

Package signature

Checksum is mandatory; cryptographic signing is decided by the public-scale threat model.

§13

Source basis and document relationship

This brief is a derivative summary. For implementation disputes, tests, schema detail, exact screen acceptance criteria and source citations, use the controlled CDR v1.1 and Appendix G.

Primary technical sourcesCCSDS OMM, CelesTrak, Space-Track, SGP4 reference implementation and platform documentation.
Design and assurance sourcesWCAG 2.2 / WCAG2ICT, Apple HIG, Material guidance, WAI-ARIA and OWASP MASVS.
Publication note

Competitor pricing, app-store economics and market figures should be revalidated immediately before investor, press or public use. Technical and policy decisions remain subject to provider, platform and external professional review where the CDR marks them as open or gated.

Modelled positions. Explicit uncertainty. Personal orbital curation.
